title: Port CUDA custom extension to NPU-routed C++/ATen extension without changing the Python API
problem_description: The project had a CUDA custom-op extension surface, and Phase 5 identified an operator failure where the entry script was valid but implementation/build artifacts and custom-op runtime evidence were not closed. This matters for CUDA-to-NPU migrations because deleting the extension, using CPU fallback, or only doing Phase 4 text replacements leaves large-model workloads unable to run natively on NPU.
rough_fix_approach: 1. Replace CUDAExtension/nvcc build assumptions with a CppExtension build over C++ sources. 2. Reimplement CUDA kernel wrapper functions as C++ ATen tensor operations that require NPU/PrivateUse1 tensors and keep data on NPU. 3. Preserve the existing pybind symbols and Python torch.autograd.Function/public API calls so downstream project code continues to import the same extension module. 4. Build the project-local shared object in place, verify exported symbols, run the validation entry script, and require per-unit runtime coverage, parity, performance, compiled-artifact evidence, and no CPU/CUDA routing before declaring success.

involved_code_files.role: Build configuration — uses torch.utils.cpp_extension.CppExtension over C++ sources instead of CUDAExtension/nvcc
Custom operator input guard — enforces NPU/PrivateUse1 tensors and contiguous inputs
Migrated custom operator implementation — NPU-routed C++/ATen implementations for gather, sampling, interpolation, grouping, and ball query wrappers
pybind11 binding layer — preserves exported native symbols on pointnet2_ops._ext
Public/autograd API layer — routes existing Python APIs to the migrated _ext symbols
NPU validation support — imports torch_npu and rejects CPU fallback before custom-op validation
Migration validation entry — requires torch_npu and records per-unit NPU runtime, parity, performance, artifact, and no-fallback evidence

reasoning: This experience is generalizable to CUDA custom extensions whose operator semantics can be expressed through NPU-supported ATen tensor operations while preserving pybind and Python API compatibility. It is verified by final validation exit_code=0, FULL_PASS, compiled shared-object evidence, all 9 units executing on runtime_device=npu, and repeated no CPU/CUDA routing evidence. Confidence is below 0.9 because projects requiring bespoke Ascend C kernels cannot use the ATen-wrapper approach as a drop-in implementation strategy.
